title: Chapter 22: The Broker
content: Mia had been looking for the data broker for six weeks.

She had started the search the day she arrived at Serenity Peak, hunched over a smuggled laptop in the dark, tracing the breadcrumbs of her own medical records. She had continued the search after the release, working through the night while the others slept.
Now she had a name.
Derek Porter. Age 34. Address: a condo in the suburbs. Occupation: "healthcare data consultant" — which meant, in practice, that he bought and sold patient records to anyone who paid.
Chloe found her at the kitchen table at 2:00 AM, staring at a photo of a man walking out of a coffee shop.
"Who is that?"
"Derek Porter. The man who sold my medical records to Serenity Peak."
Chloe sat down across from her. "You found him."
"I found him. The question is what to do with him."
"You could go to the police," Chloe said.

Mia shook her head. "The police won't do anything. What he did is technically legal — there's a loophole in data privacy laws that allows brokers to sell 'de-identified' records. Except the records he sold weren't de-identified. Names, addresses, insurance codes, family contact info — everything."
"So it's illegal."

"It's illegal. But proving he knew the records were being used for false imprisonment is harder. He'll say he sold them to a legitimate medical facility. He'll say he didn't know what they were doing there."
"Do you believe that?"
Mia looked at the photo. The man in the photo looked ordinary. Glasses. A casual jacket. He could have been anyone.
"No."
"Then let's go see him."
Mia looked up. "Now?"
"It's 2:00 AM. Guilty people sleep lighter at 2:00 AM. That's when they tell the truth."
Derek Porter lived in a condo complex twenty minutes from the city center.

Mia parked across the street and killed the engine. The building was beige and boxy, one of those developments that looked identical to every other development. Unit 204. Second floor, end of the hall.
"What's the plan?" Chloe asked.
"I knock. He opens. I talk."
"And if he doesn't open?"
"Then I knock louder."
They walked up the stairs. The hallway was quiet. A single light flickered overhead.
Mia stopped at Unit 204. She took a breath. Then she knocked.
Thirty seconds passed. Nothing.
She knocked again. Harder.
A voice from inside. Muffled. Annoyed. "Who is it?"
"Mia Chen. You sold my medical records to Serenity Peak Wellness Center. I want to talk."
Silence. Then the sound of locks clicking.
The door opened.
Derek Porter stood in the doorway, squinting against the hallway light. He was shorter than his photo suggested. Pale. Wearing a t-shirt and sleep pants.
He looked at Mia. Then at Chloe. Then back at Mia.
"I was wondering when you'd show up."
Mia stepped inside.
The apartment was clean but sparse. A couch. A TV. A desk with two monitors. The kind of space that belonged to someone who spent most of his time online.
Derek closed the door. He did not look nervous. He looked resigned.
"You know why I'm here," Mia said.
"I have a pretty good idea."
"You sold my medical records to a facility that used them to lock me up."

Derek ran a hand through his hair. "Look. I'm not going to pretend I didn't. But I want you to understand something — I didn't know what they were doing with the data. I sold them patient records. Thousands of them. I didn't vet every buyer."
"You didn't vet them?"
"It's a volume business. I process requests, match buyers to sellers, take a cut. I don't ask questions."
"That's your defense?"
"It's not a defense. It's the truth. I made a choice not to look too closely. I knew some of my buyers were sketchy. I looked the other way. That's on me."
Mia studied him. He was not trying to lie. That was unexpected.
"Why are you being honest?" she asked.
Derek sat down on the couch. He looked tired.

"Because I saw the news. I saw what happened at Serenity Peak. I saw the testimonies. And I realized that somewhere in the chain of people who put you in that place, there was a guy sitting in a condo, processing data requests, not asking questions."
He looked up at her.
"That guy was me."
Mia stood in the middle of the room, processing.
She had expected a fight. She had expected lies, excuses, a slammed door. She had not expected this — a man who looked at his own reflection and did not like what he saw.
"What do you want me to do?" Derek asked. "I'll testify. I'll give you everything I have on Serenity Peak's data purchases. I'll help you trace every record they bought."
"Why?"
"Because I can't undo what I did. But I can make sure it doesn't happen again."
Mia looked at Chloe. Chloe said nothing.
"Okay," Mia said. "But you're doing it my way."
"What's your way?"

"You're going to cooperate with the investigation. You're going to hand over all your records. And when this is over, you're going to shut down your business and work with a nonprofit that helps people whose data was exploited."
Derek was quiet for a moment.
"That's fair," he said.
They walked out of the apartment at 3:30 AM.
The sky was still dark. The street was empty.
"I didn't expect that," Mia said.
"Neither did I."
"I thought I would hate him. I thought I would want to destroy him."
"And now?"
Mia looked back at the building. Unit 204. The light was on.
"I still want to destroy the system that made his business possible," she said. "But him? I think he's going to do more good as an ally than as a target."
Chloe smiled. "That's growth."
"That's strategy."
"Both."
Mia did not sleep that night.

She sat at her workstation when she got home, staring at the files Derek had sent her. A complete record of every transaction he had processed for Serenity Peak. Names. Dates. Amounts. And — crucially — the email chain where the facility's purchasing agent had specifically requested "records with family contact information included."
That was the smoking gun.
He had asked for family contacts. He had known exactly what he was buying.
Mia saved the file, encrypted it, and sent a copy to Elena.
Then she closed her laptop and lay down on the floor.
The ceiling was white and blank. She stared at it until her eyes blurred.
Six weeks ago, she had been in a room with barred windows, wondering if she would ever see her laptop again. Now she had a name, a confession, and a lawsuit that would set a national precedent.
Not bad for a former hacker.
She smiled in the dark.
Then she closed her eyes and slept.
The next morning, Chloe found an email from Elena with the subject line: *We have them.*
The attachment was the email chain. Chloe read it twice. Then she printed it and pinned it to the whiteboard next to the facility renovation plans.
When Mia walked into the war room at noon, the others were already there. Tiffany. Amara. Elena. They were standing around the whiteboard, reading the printed emails.
"You did this?" Tiffany asked.
Mia looked at the board. At the email chain that would seal the case. At the women who had become her family.
"We did this," she said.
